title: Quantum Phase Estimation

theory.introduction: Quantum Phase Estimation (QPE) is one of the most important quantum algorithms. It estimates the phase (eigenvalue) associated with an eigenvector of a unitary operator. QPE forms the foundation of many advanced algorithms including Shor's algorithm and Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE).
key_points: QPE estimates eigenvalues of unitary operators
Uses Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) as a core subroutine
Precision depends on number of counting qubits
Transforms phase information into measurable probabilities

mathematical_representation.phase_range: φ is a real value between 0 and 1
mathematical_representation.precision_relation: More counting qubits → higher phase precision

intuition.description: Phase Estimation extracts hidden phase information encoded in a quantum state. The algorithm converts phase differences into measurable probabilities using interference patterns created by QFT.
intuition.core_idea: Convert phase information into computational basis states that can be measured.

registers.counting_register: n qubits initialized in superposition using Hadamard gates
registers.eigenstate_register: qubit prepared in eigenstate |ψ⟩ of unitary operator U

steps.description: Apply Hadamard gates to counting register creating superposition
Apply controlled-U operations with exponentially increasing powers
Apply inverse QFT on counting register
Measure counting register to obtain binary phase estimate

circuit_examples.name: Basic Phase Estimation Circuit
circuit_examples.description: Uses 3 counting qubits to estimate phase

circuit_examples.name: Example with Phase Gate
circuit_examples.description: Estimate phase of a simple phase rotation gate

phase_kickback.description: Controlled unitary operations transfer phase information to counting qubits.

interference.description: Inverse QFT converts phase differences into measurable probability peaks.

precision.relationship: Precision increases exponentially with number of counting qubits

connection_to_qft.role: Inverse QFT extracts binary representation of phase
connection_to_qft.dependency: QPE relies on efficient implementation of QFT

summary: Quantum Phase Estimation efficiently extracts eigenvalue information encoded as phase in quantum states. It is a fundamental building block for many powerful quantum algorithms.

simulation.expected_result: Phase information extracted from counting register
try_this: Qubit 0 acts as the counting register; qubit 1 as the eigenstate register
The controlled-CNOT transfers phase from the eigenstate to the counting qubit
The inverse QFT (simplified here as H) converts phase into a measurable probability

quiz.question: What does Quantum Phase Estimation estimate?
options: The phase (eigenvalue) of a unitary operator
quiz.answer: The phase (eigenvalue) of a unitary operator
quiz.explanation: QPE estimates the phase φ in the eigenvalue equation U|ψ⟩ = e^{2πiφ}|ψ⟩.

quiz.question: How does precision improve in QPE?
quiz.explanation: Each additional counting qubit doubles the precision of the phase estimate.

quiz.question: Which algorithm is QPE a core component of?
quiz.explanation: Shor's factoring algorithm relies on QPE to extract the period needed for factorization.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of the inverse QFT in QPE?
options: To convert phase information into measurable computational basis states
quiz.answer: To convert phase information into measurable computational basis states
quiz.explanation: The inverse QFT maps the phase encoded in the counting register into a binary number that can be measured.
